model_narrative: We ship a product from a set of canning plants to a set of markets. For each plant and market pair we decide how many cases to ship. We also track the unused supply at each plant and the surplus delivered at each market, and we carry binary indicators that mark which shipments and slacks are allowed into the basis. The objective is to minimize the total transportation cost over all lanes.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the canning plants that ship product, that is the supply origins

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the markets that receive product, that is the demand destinations

params.name: a
params.doc: the supply capacity available at each plant, in cases

params.name: b
params.doc: the demand required at each market, in cases

params.name: d
params.doc: the distance from each plant to each market, in thousands of miles

params.name: f
params.doc: the freight rate, in dollars per case per thousand miles

params.name: c
params.doc: the transport cost per case shipped from each plant to each market, in thousands of dollars per case; it equals the freight rate times the distance divided by one thousand

params.name: min_ab
params.doc: for each plant and market, the smaller of that plant's supply capacity and that market's demand, in cases; it serves as the largest shipment that could ever flow on that lane and is used as the big-M upper bound for the corresponding shipment

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity shipped from each plant to each market, in cases

vars.name: sslack
vars.doc: the unused supply at each plant, that is the supply slack, in cases

vars.name: dslack
vars.doc: the excess delivered above the demand at each market, that is the demand slack, in cases

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all lanes, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: xind
vars.doc: a binary indicator that is one when the shipment on a lane is allowed to be a basic variable and zero when it is forced out of the basis

vars.name: sslind
vars.doc: a binary indicator that is one when a plant's supply slack is allowed to be a basic variable and zero when it is forced out of the basis

vars.name: dslind
vars.doc: a binary indicator that is one when a market's demand slack is allowed to be a basic variable and zero when it is forced out of the basis

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost gathers the cost of every shipment across all lanes. For each plant and market pair, value the cases shipped on that lane at that lane's per case transport cost, and set the total cost variable equal to the sum of these amounts over every plant and market pair.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each plant cannot ship out more than it has available, and any capacity it does not use is recorded as unused supply. For each plant, the total cases shipped from that plant to all markets must equal its supply capacity less its unused supply.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each market must receive at least the cases it demands, and anything delivered beyond that is recorded as surplus. For each market, the total cases shipped into that market from all plants must equal its demand plus its surplus.

constraints.name: i)
constraints.intent: The number of variables marked as basic is fixed by the size of the problem. Across all the shipment indicators, all the supply slack indicators, and all the demand slack indicators, the count that are switched on must equal the number of plants plus the number of markets.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: A shipment can only flow on a lane when that lane's indicator is switched on. For each plant and market pair, the cases shipped on that lane must not exceed that lane's largest possible flow when the indicator is on, and must be zero when the indicator is off.

constraints.name: sslack[i]
constraints.intent: A plant's unused supply can only be positive when its supply slack indicator is switched on. For each plant, the unused supply must not exceed that plant's supply capacity when the indicator is on, and must be zero when the indicator is off.

constraints.name: dslack[j]
constraints.intent: A market's surplus can only be positive when its demand slack indicator is switched on. For each market, the surplus must not exceed that market's demand when the indicator is on, and must be zero when the indicator is off.

query: Our rail broker came back with exclusive-OR terms on two corridors: we can keep the Seattle-to-Chicago lane active in the plan or the San-Diego-to-Topeka lane active, but not both — at most one of those two lanes gets flagged on. What does the optimizer return for total cost under that pick-one rule?

query: Sales is pitching a two-part commitment for next quarter, and both pieces have to hold at the same time: anchor New York with at least 300 cases shipped out of Seattle, and keep a Seattle toehold in Topeka of at least 25 cases so the broker there stays warm. Where does total shipping cost land with both commitments in place?

query: There's a proposal to commit San Diego to the eastern corridor: its deliveries into Chicago and New York, added together, would have to come to at least 400 cases. What does the optimizer come back with for total cost under that commitment?

query: New York is our single biggest market, and San Diego ends up owning nearly all of it while Seattle barely shows up there. Shouldn't Seattle be putting at least as many cases into New York as San Diego does? What's the model seeing that keeps the split that lopsided?

query: Chicago and Topeka are Seattle's natural inland territory, yet its combined volume into those two markets sits at just 300 cases while the plant has 350 of capacity. Shouldn't Seattle's Chicago and Topeka deliveries add up to at least 340 cases? What's the tradeoff that keeps the model from loading up our inland plant?

query: Topeka is one hundred percent dependent on San Diego right now — if that plant hiccups, Topeka goes dark. Shouldn't San Diego's deliveries into Topeka be capped at 70% of the combined cases both plants put into that market, so Seattle always carries the rest? What's keeping the optimizer from spreading that risk?
